questions.q: Paragraph: A nichrome wire $A B, 100 \mathrm{~cm}$ long and of uniform cross section is mounted on a meter scale, the ${ }_1$ and B coinciding with 0 cm and 100 cm marks respectively. The wire has a resistance $S=50 \mathrm{ohm}$. A $C$ along this wire, between $A$ and $B$ is called a variable point to which one end of an electrical ele connected. In the following questions this arrangement will be referred to as 'wire $A B$ '.Question: The 'wire AB ' is now a part of the adjacent circuit. When the resistors are interchanged, the null point is found at C with $\mathrm{AC}=67 \mathrm{~cm}$. The systematic error in this experiment seems to be due to noncoincidence of A and B with 0 cm mark and 100 cm mark respectively. questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q: Paragraph: The following questions are concerned with experiments on the characterization and use of a moving coil galvanometer. The series combination of a variable resistance $R$, one $100 \Omega$ resistor and a moving coil galvanometer is connected to a mobile phone charger having negligible internal resistance. The zero of the galvanometer lies at the centre and the pointer can move 30 divisions full scale on either side depending on the direction of current. The reading of the galvanometer is 10 divisions and the voltages across the galvanometer and $100 \Omega$ resistor are respectively 12 mV and 16 mV .Question: The resistance of the galvanometer in ohm is

questions.q: Paragraph: The following questions are concerned with experiments on the characterization and use of a moving coil galvanometer. The series combination of a variable resistance $R$, one $100 \Omega$ resistor and a moving coil galvanometer is connected to a mobile phone charger having negligible internal resistance. The zero of the galvanometer lies at the centre and the pointer can move 30 divisions full scale on either side depending on the direction of current. The reading of the galvanometer is 10 divisions and the voltages across the galvanometer and $100 \Omega$ resistor are respectively 12 mV and 16 mV . questions.q: In the circuit arrangement shown two cells supply a current $I$ to a load resistance $R=9 \Omega$. One cell has an $\operatorname{emf} E_1=9 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r_1=1 \Omega$ and the other cell has an emf $E_2=6 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r_2=3 \Omega$. The currents are as shown. Then,
options: if the cell of emf $E_1$ is removed, current I will be smaller.
if the cell of emf $E_2$ is removed, current I will be smaller.

questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q: Each of 9 sides of frame A CD E F B has resistance $R$ (Nine in all) A current $I$ enters at A and leaves at B. Choose the correct alternatives.
options: Currents in branches CD and EF are zero.
Currents in branches CE and DF are each equal to $\frac{4}{11} \mathrm{I}$
Effective resistance between $A$ and $B$ is $\frac{15}{11} R$
Effective resistance between A and B is $\frac{3}{4} R$

questions.subject: NSEP
questions.q: In the circuit shown, the current in the $8 \Omega$ resistance across G and H is $i=0.5$ ampere. The ammeter is ideal. The internal resistance of the cell is $0.8 \Omega$. Choose correct option(s).
options: Reading of the ammeter is 1.5 ampere
Potential difference across A and H is 13 V
Potential difference across C and F is 9 V
The emf of the cell is 24 V
